coredata.dc:title: A novel dual-function fluorescence sensor Eu/CDs@MOF-808 for the sensitive detection of adenosine triphosphate and uric acid
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Liping
Guo, Hao
Ren, Borong
Hui, Yingfei
Zhuang, Dengke
Liu, Yu
Yan, Ruixue
Niu, Wenting
Yang, Wu
coredata.dc:description: Designing and developing a reliable method for the detection of potential biomarker molecules are crucial for prevention and early diagnosis of diseases. In this study, we present a novel functionalized metal–organic framework material (MOF-808) that is developed to construct ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform (Eu/CDs@MOF-808) for the detection of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and uric acid (UA). The fluorescent probe Eu/CDs@MOF-808 prepared through the in-situ encapsulation of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) within the structure of MOF-808 exhibits excellent optical properties. Additionally, rare earth ions (Eu3+) are incorporated into the CDs@MOF-808 using a post-synthetic modification strategy. The presence of ATP significantly enhances the characteristic peak of carbon dots (CDs) at 440 nm, while the peak of Eu3+ at 614 nm remains relatively unchanged. Furthermore, the concentration of ATP exhibits a strong linear relationship with the ratiometric fluorescence intensity (F440/F614) in the concentration range of 0.5–450 μM, with a detection limit of 0.22 μM. Notably, the addition of uric acid (UA) to this fluorescence sensing platform leads to a marked reduction in the characteristic peak intensity of Eu3+, which results in a satisfactory linear relationship between the ratiometric fluorescence intensity and UA concentration in the concentration ranges of 0.04–300 μM and 300–650 μM, with a detection limit of 0.04 μM. Additionally, the fluorescent sensor has been successfully applied for the detection of ATP and UA in real serum and urine samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal organic frames
Post-synthetic modification
Multi-functional fluorescent sensor
Biomarker

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of ATP and UA in human serum and urine
Characterization of MOF-808, CDs@MOF-808 and Eu/CDs@MOF-808
Analytical performance of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 toward ATP
Analytical performance of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 toward UA
Selectivity of the probe material for ATP and UA
Detection of ATP and UA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The TEM image of CDs, (b) the particle size distribution of CDs.
SEM images of MOF-808 (a-c), CDs@MOF-808 (d-f) and Eu/CDs@MOF-808 (g-i) at different magnifications.
(a,b) TEM images of Eu/CDs@MOF-808, (c) EDS elemental mapping of Eu/CDs@MOF-808.

(a) XRD patterns of simulated MOF-808 and synthesized MOF-808, CDs@MOF-808, Eu/CDs@MOF-808 powder, (b) XRD spectra of the composite Eu/CDs@MOF-808 after the addition of different masses of CDs, (c) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and its pore size distribution of MOF-808, (d) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and its pore size distribution of Eu/CDs@MOF-808, (e) XPS survey spectra of MOF-808 and Eu/CDs@MOF-808, (f) high-resolution XPS scan of Eu 3d in Eu/CDs@ MOF-808.

The fluorescence emission spectra of different materials under the same conditions (λex = 290 nm, slit width = 5) (a) and the fluorescence emission spectra of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 at different pH (b), NaCl concentrations (c) and storage time (d).

(a)Fluorescence response of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 toward to different ATP concentrations, (b) Linear response of relative fluorescence intensity to ATP concentration, (c) Relative fluorescence intensity of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 at different response times after addition of ATP, (d) Fluorescence response of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 to interfering analytes in the presence and absence of ATP.

(a) Fluorescence response of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 to different concentrations of UA, (b) linear response of relative fluorescence intensity to UA concentration, (c) relative fluorescence intensity of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 at different response times after addition of UA, (d) fluorescence response of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 to interferents in the presence and absence of UA.

(a) ATP concentration-dependent fluorescence emission spectra of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 in the presence of UA, (b) linear response of relative fluorescence intensity to ATP concentration, (c) UA concentration-dependent fluorescence emission spectra of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 in the presence of ATP, (d) linear response of relative fluorescence intensity to UA concentration.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 in the presence of some amino acids or biomolecules, (b) fluorescence emission spectra of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 in the presence or absence of ATP and UA.

(a) FTIR spectra of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 and Eu/CDs@MOF-808 + ATP, (b) HUMO and LUMO energy levels of H3BDC and UA, (c) UV absorption spectrum of UA (red line), ATP (purple) and excitation spectrum of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 (black line), (d) fluorescence lifetimes before and after adding UA. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic diagram of the preparation process of the dual-emission probe.

head.ce:title: A novel dual-function fluorescence sensor Eu/CDs@MOF-808 for the sensitive detection of adenosine triphosphate and uric acid

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-functional Polymer Materials of the Ministry of Education, Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Gansu International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Water-Retention Chemical Functional Materials, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-functional Polymer Materials of the Ministry of Education

College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Gansu International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Water-Retention Chemical Functional Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Eco-functional Polymer Materials of the Ministry of Education, Key Lab of Bioelectrochemistry and Environmental Analysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Northwest Normal University, Gansu International Scientific and Technological Cooperation Base of Water-Retention Chemical Functional Materials, Lanzhou 730070, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Designing and developing a reliable method for the detection of potential biomarker molecules are crucial for prevention and early diagnosis of diseases. In this study, we present a novel functionalized metal–organic framework material (MOF-808) that is developed to construct ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform (Eu/CDs@MOF-808) for the detection of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and uric acid (UA). The fluorescent probe Eu/CDs@MOF-808 prepared through the in-situ encapsulation of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) within the structure of MOF-808 exhibits excellent optical properties. Additionally, rare earth ions (Eu3+) are incorporated into the CDs@MOF-808 using a post-synthetic modification strategy. The presence of ATP significantly enhances the characteristic peak of carbon dots (CDs) at 440 nm, while the peak of Eu3+ at 614 nm remains relatively unchanged. Furthermore, the concentration of ATP exhibits a strong linear relationship with the ratiometric fluorescence intensity (F440/F614) in the concentration range of 0.5–450 μM, with a detection limit of 0.22 μM. Notably, the addition of uric acid (UA) to this fluorescence sensing platform leads to a marked reduction in the characteristic peak intensity of Eu3+, which results in a satisfactory linear relationship between the ratiometric fluorescence intensity and UA concentration in the concentration ranges of 0.04–300 μM and 300–650 μM, with a detection limit of 0.04 μM. Additionally, the fluorescent sensor has been successfully applied for the detection of ATP and UA in real serum and urine samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is a high-energy compound primarily involved in human metabolism. It facilitates substance metabolism and synthesis, provides energy supply, promotes cellular repair and regeneration, and enhances metabolic activity [1–3]. Monitoring ATP concentration is essential, as it is linked to various diseases, including Parkinson's disease, hypoglycemia, and cardiovascular disease [4,5]. So, ATP is considered as an important biomarker for indicating these diseases. Consequently, the development of detection methods with high sensitivity and selectivity for efficiently monitoring of ATP concentration is crucial for disease diagnosis and early recognition [6,7].

Uric acid (UA) is the principal metabolic product of purine in the body, and its levels serve as biomarkers for various diseases [8,9]. Normal serum UA levels have been reported in the range from 0.12 to 0.46 mM, while healthy uric acid concentrations in urine are between 1.4 and 4.4 mM [10]. In contemporary society, as living standards continue to improve, the intake of high-purine and high-energy foods has also increased [11], leading to elevated UA levels in the body. Excessive UA concentrations in body fluids can result in various human disorders, including myocardial fibrosis [12], gout [13], hypertension [14], kidney disease [15], and endothelial dysfunction [16]. Therefore, the development of a highly sensitive, rapid, reliable, and straightforward biosensor for the efficient detection of UA is essential.

Up to now, numerous approaches for the analysis of ATP and UA have been published, including colorimetry [17], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [18], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [19] and electrochemical methods [20]. Although these techniques are widely utilized, they each have inherent limitations. Specifically, most methods necessitate costly instrumentation. Additionally, despite their high sensitivity, they can be time-consuming, complex, and often require laborious sample pre-treatment [21]. In contrast, the fluorescence detection method is widely utilized due to its advantages of simplicity, convenience, and cost-effectiveness [22]. However, the emission intensity can be disturbed by many factors including the drifts of the lamps and detectors, the probe concentration, and autofluorescence in complicated biosystems. The above factors are prone to disturbance in quantitative determination. Fortunately, ratiometric fluorescence sensors, incorporating a built-in self-calibration feature by recording fluorescence intensities at two different wavelengths, which serve as reference and response signals, respectively, can effectively reduce background interference and enhance the accuracy of detection [23]. Consequently, numerous researchers are developing functional materials based on metal–organic frameworks (MOFs) for the construction of ratiometric fluorescent sensors using synthetic post-modification methods [24]. MOFs are a class of porous crystalline materials consisting of metal ions or metal clusters and organic ligands by coordination bonds [25,26]. Due to their large specific surface area, adjustable pore size and facile functionalisation, they have been extensively utilized in applications such as catalysis [27], gas separation [28], sensing [29], and drug delivery [30], among others.

In recent years, researchers have developed luminescent MOFs by incorporating fluorophores and rare earth ions (Eu3+, Tb3+) into their structures [24,31]. For example, Liu et al. [32] designed a fluorescent sensor aimed at the sensitive detection of 2,4,6-trinitrophenol by combining magnesium, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (Mg, N-CDs) with red-emitting cadmium telluride (r-CdTe) within the UiO-66 framework. Carbon dots (CDs) represent an innovative category of carbon-based fluorescent materials, extensively applied across a range of fields, including biofluorescence imaging, fluorescence sensing, clinical therapy, and optoelectronic devices, owing to their outstanding optical characteristics, superior biocompatibility, and remarkable stability [33–35]. It has been noted that the majority of sensors that utilize CDs rely on a single emission center, which may result in detection inaccuracies due to fluctuations in external conditions. As a result, ratiometric fluorescent sensors can be designed by incorporating two or more emission centers within MOFs. Furthermore, the ability to self-calibrate improves detection precision, leading to enhanced selectivity and sensitivity in the produced luminescent MOFs. Therefore, the construction of ratiometric fluorescent probes based on functionalized MOFs through post-synthesis modification strategies stands out as a highly promising research avenue [36].

In this study, a composite material named Eu/CDs@MOFs-808 was synthesized through the in-situ growth method, where CDs were encapsulated by MOF-808 as the base framework. Subsequently, rare earth ions Eu3+ were incorporated via a post-synthetic modification technique as outlined in Scheme 1 . This composite was then employed to develop ratiometric fluorescent sensor aimed at detecting ATP and UA. The emission peak of the CDs, positioned at 440 nm, exhibited a notable increase in intensity upon the addition of ATP, whereas the characteristic peak of Eu3+ (614 nm) showed negligible change. In contrast, the introduction of UA into the Eu/CD@MOF-808 fluorescent probe system led to a substantial reduction in fluorescence intensity at 614 nm, with the characteristic peaks of CDs remaining stable. Additionally, the Eu/CD@MOFs-808 probe was utilized to assess ATP and UA levels in serum and urine, evaluating its capability for sensing real samples. This research offers a groundbreaking strategy for the effective detection of ATP and UA, which could support clinicians in the timely diagnosis and prevention of specific diseases.
CDs were fabricated via a hydrothermal approach which was based on the reported literature [37] (Supporting Information).
MOF-808 was synthesised by modifying the method of preceding researchers [38] (Supporting Information).
The preparation of CDs@MOF-808 was essentially similar to that of MOF-808. The difference is that CDs@MOF-808 was synthesised by adding 10 mg CDs.
Eu/CDs@MOF-808 was prepared by post-synthetic modification according to the literature [39] (Supporting Information).

To detect ATP, a mixture was created by combining 500 μL of 1 mg/mL Eu/CDs@MOF-808 suspension with different concentrations of ATP solution ranging from 0 to 1000 μM, achieving a final total volume of 5 mL. This mixture was left to incubate at room temperature for 10 min, then the fluorescence emission spectra were collected. For the detection of UA, different concentrations of UA solutions were added to 500 μL of 1 mg/mL suspension of Eu/CDs@MOF-808, followed by recording of the fluorescence emission spectra.

To evaluate the selectivity of the developed fluorescent sensor for ATP and UA, the fluorescence responses of various compounds were analyzed, including arginine (Arg), cysteine (Cys), phenylalanine (Phe), glutamine (Glu), D-leucine (D-Leu), L-leucine (L-Leu), threonine (Thr), leucine (Leu), glycine (Gly), D-valine (D-Val), L-valine (L-Val), D-histidine (D-His), L-histidine (L-His), creatinine (Cre), adenosine monophosphate (AMP), adenosine diphosphate (ADP), pyrophosphate (PPi) and some metal ions. Specifically, 500 μL (1 mg/mL) of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 was combined with 10-3 M target analyte and thoroughly mixed through sonication. The fluorescence spectra were subsequently recorded following a 10 min incubation period. Additionally, to assess the sensor's anti-interference capabilities, fluorescence intensity was measured in the presence of ATP or UA interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of ATP and UA in human serum and urine
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the practical application potential of the Eu/CDs@MOF-808 sensor, the developed fluorescent probe was utilized for the detection of ATP and UA in human serum and urine (samples were collected from a healthy volunteer). Initially, the serum sample underwent pre-treatment following established protocols in the literature [40]. Furthermore, the urine sample was filtered using a 0.22 μm membrane and subsequently diluted 100-fold with deionized water, in accordance with previously reported methodologies [41]. For analysis, 1 mL of serum or urine was mixed with distilled water to reach a final volume of 100 mL. Various concentrations (20, 40, 60 μM) of the analytes were added to the respective sample, fluorescence spectra were recorded, and the concentrations of ATP and UA within the sample were calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of MOF-808, CDs@MOF-808 and Eu/CDs@MOF-808
ce:section.ce:para: MOF-808 was synthesized using a straightforward solvothermal approach. Additionally, the formation of CDs@MOF-808 involved the incorporation of CDs into MOF-808 through in situ growth. Notably, the composite material Eu/CDs@MOF-808 was generated by integrating rare earth ions (Eu3+) into the CDs@MOF-808 structure via a post-synthetic modification technique. First, the morphological characteristics of the CDs were examined through transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As illustrated in Fig. 1 a, the TEM image indicated that the CDs were evenly distributed. Furthermore, the statistical analysis of the particle sizes of the CDs is presented in Fig. 1b. It is evident that the particle size distribution for the carbon dots ranges from 1.5 to 15.9 nm, with an average particle size of 7.5 nm.

The morphologies of MOF-808, CDs@MOF-808, and Eu/CDs@MOF-808 were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). As shown in Fig. 2 a–c, the SEM images revealed that pure MOF-808 exhibited a regular octahedral shape with a uniform distribution. The incorporation of CDs into MOF-808 resulted in a slight alteration of its morphology and a rougher surface. However, the integrity of the octahedral framework remained intact, as depicted in Fig. 2d–f. As illustrated in Fig. 2g–i, the morphology of the composite obtained after the two-step modification remained unchanged and the structure of the ortho-octahedron was still present.

To further investigate the morphology of the target material Eu/CDs@MOFs-808, TEM tests were conducted as in Fig. 3 a, b. It is evident that the parent framework still exhibits an ortho-octahedral structure in the composites after the two-step modification, with CDs distributed throughout the Eu/CDs@MOFs-808 matrix. The uniform distribution of elements C, Zr, N, Cu, Eu and O can be observed in the DES mapping of Fig. 3c. Furthermore, the molar ratio of Zr4+/Eu3+/Cu2+ was approximately 10.4:1.2:1, as indicated by the ICP-OES measurements in Table S1, further confirming that CDs and Eu3+ were successfully incorporated into MOF-808.

The crystal structures of MOFs-808, CDs@MOFs-808, and Eu/CDs@MOFs-808 were confirmed through their X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, the XRD patterns of the synthesized MOFs-808, CDs@MOFs-808, and Eu/CDs@MOFs-808 exhibited major diffraction peaks that closely resemble the simulated peaks [42]. This similarity indicated that the successful construction of MOFs-808 and the incorporation of CDs and Eu3+ had minimal impact on its crystal structure. Furthermore, the XRD patterns of the composite Eu/CDs@MOF-808, with varying amounts of CDs were compared in Fig. 4b. It was evident that the XRD pattern of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 showed negligible changes with the addition of CDs, further confirming that introduction of CDs did not significantly affect the XRD characteristic peaks of MOF-808. Additionally, as depicted in Fig. 4c and Fig. 4d, the specific surface area measured by BET decreased from the original 1984.1 m2/g to 653.9 m2/g, while the mean pore size reduced from 3.64 nm to 2.96 nm following the gradual modification by CDs and Eu3+.

The investigation of the structures and functional groups present in CDs and MOF-808 was conducted using FT-IR spectroscopy, as shown in Fig. S1. The FT-IR spectra showed the characteristic peaks of CDs at 3246 cm−1 and 2967 cm−1, which were associated with the stretching vibrations of O-H and C-H groups, respectively. Significant absorption peaks at 1661.9 cm−1 and 1553.7 cm−1 corresponded to the vibrations of C=O and C=N. Furthermore, the absorption peak at 1126.1 cm-1 resulted from C-O stretching vibrations, while the peak at 629.1 cm−1 was attributed to the bending vibration of C-H. Notably, the FT-IR spectra of CDs@MOF-808 showed enhanced peaks at 1661.9 cm−1 and 1553.7 cm−1 compared to those found in MOF-808, confirming the successful integration of CDs into the MOF-808 structure. Compared to primitive MOF-808, the XPS spectrum for Eu/CDs@MOF-808 revealed new peaks at 402.4 eV, 954.5 eV, and 1134.5 eV (Fig. 4e), which corresponded to characteristic peaks of N 1 s, Cu 2p, and Eu 3d, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of Eu 3d, depicted in Fig. 4f, exhibited two prominent peaks with binding energies of 1133.9 eV and 1165.1 eV, which were attributed to 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 spin-orbital components, respectively [43]. These results, in conjunction with the FT-IR findings, validated the successful incorporation of CDs and Eu3+ into MOF-808. Subsequently, the thermal stability of the composites was evaluated before and after the inclusion of CDs. As illustrated in Fig. S2, thermogravimetric analysis demonstrated that MOF-808 possessed high thermal stability, and introduction of CDs almost did not influence the thermal decomposition temperature.

Fig. S3 displayed the Eu/CDs@MOF-808 composite exhibited two distinct types of emissions when excited at 290 nm. The emission peak at 440 nm corresponded to the characteristic peak of CDs, while the emission peaks at 580, 590, 614, 650, and 697 nm were attributed to the characteristic emissions of Eu3+ ions (5D0 → 7FJ (J = 0–4)). A distinct characteristic emission peak at 614 nm was observed corresponding to the 5D0-7F2 transition, indicating that the “antenna” effect successfully occurred. That is, the electron of the ligand (H3BTC) is first excited to its S1 state and transferred to the T1 state via intersystem crossing. Then Eu3+ ion shows its characteristic emission when the energy is transferred from the T1 state of the ligand to the Eu3+ ion through a nonradiative process [31]. The fluorescence emission spectra of different materials under identical conditions (with an excitation wavelength of 290 nm and slit widths of 5 nm) were presented in Fig. 5 a. It was evident that pure MOF-808 only displayed a weak and broad fluorescence emission peak in the range of 375–550 nm. In contrast, the CDs solution exhibited a stronger fluorescence emission. Notably, the fluorescence emission of the resulting composite, CDs@MOF-808, was significantly enhanced when CDs were in-situ encapsulated within MOF-808, thereby confirming the successful incorporation of CDs. Furthermore, the emission spectrum of the target composite, Eu/CDs@MOF-808, included not only the peak of CDs at 440 nm but also the characteristic peak of Eu3+ at 614 nm. These experimental results all demonstrated the successful preparation of the composites.

The optical stability of the Eu/CDs@MOF-808 probe was investigated under various extreme conditions. Initially, the fluorescence emission spectra of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 at different pH were recorded in Fig. 5b. The relative emission intensities of F440/F614 remained nearly constant in the pH range of 2 to 9, indicating that the probe is suitably used in both strongly acidic and weakly alkaline environments. Additionally, the Eu/CDs@MOF-808 was dispersed in NaCl solutions of varying concentrations, and its fluorescence emission spectra were collected (Fig. 5c). Notably, the relative fluorescence intensity exhibited only a slight decrease with increasing NaCl concentration, demonstrating that the constructed fluorescent probe possesses excellent salt tolerance. Furthermore, the ratiometric fluorescence intensities at different storage durations were compared in Fig. 5d. The fluorescence intensity of the probe remained nearly stable, with no significant decrease observed over the course of a week. Consequently, this constructed fluorescent sensor exhibited remarkable luminescence stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 toward ATP
ce:section.ce:para: Upon the addition of ATP, the fluorescent peak of CDs at 440 nm in Eu/CDs@MOF-808 was significantly augmented. With increasing ATP concentration, the characteristic peak of CDs showed a noticeable enhancement, while the peak of Eu3+ experienced a minor reduction, as depicted in Fig. 6 a. The fluorescence intensity ratio F440/F614 demonstrated remarkable linearity with ATP concentrations ranging from 0.5 to 450 μM (Fig. 6b) with a detection limit (LOD) of 0.22 μM (3Sb/K). This performance highlights a wider linear range and a lower detection limit compared to other recent sensors, as detailed in Table S2. Moreover, the response time for ATP detection was also analyzed in Fig. 6c and it was found that the response to ATP was completed in roughly 1 min, indicating that the developed fluorescent sensor was both sensitive and quick for ATP identification. Furthermore, the anti-interference capability of the fluorescence probe, Eu/CDs@MOF-808, was examined for ATP detection. In the presence of common bioanalytes and interferences with similar characteristics, including metal ions, amino acids, anions, glucose, adenosine monophosphate (AMP), adenosine diphosphate (ADP), pyrophosphate (PPi), the recorded relative fluorescence intensities in Fig. 6d indicated that these factors had a negligible impact on the response to ATP. This result emphasized the exceptional anti-interference performance of the probe Eu/CDs@MOF-808.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 toward UA
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission spectra of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 at varying concentrations of UA were recorded in Fig. 7 a. As the concentration of UA increased, the fluorescence emission peak of Eu3+ at 614 nm exhibited a pronounced decrease, while the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 440 nm remained stable. As shown in Fig. 7b, the fluorescence intensity ratio F440/F614 demonstrated a good linear relationship with UA concentration in both ranges of 0.04–300 μM (R2 = 0.948) and 300–650 μM (R2 = 0.998) and the detection limit (LOD) was estimated to be 0.04 μM based on 3Sb/K. In terms of linear range and detection limit, the Eu/CDs@MOF-808 probe is superior to most previously reported sensors of the same type in Table S3. Furthermore, the response time of UA to Eu/CDs@MOF-808 was investigated in Fig. 7c. The results revealed that the response was completed within 200 s following the addition of UA, suggesting that this probe facilitates rapid UA detection, which was advantageous for clinical applications. Additionally, to assess the anti-interference performance of the fluorescence sensor for UA detection, the fluorescence response of UA to Eu/CDs@MOF-808 in the presence of other interfering substances was recorded in Fig. 7d. It was evident that the presence of coexisting substances had a negligible effect on the detection of UA, demonstrating that the Eu/CDs@MOF-808 probe possesses satisfactory anti-interference performance for the fluorescence analysis of UA.

In addition, simultaneous detection of ATP and UA when they coexisted was explored in Fig. 8 . Considering the fact that these two analytes have completely opposite effects on the luminescence properties of the probe Eu/CDs@MOF-808, a “buffering-to-saturation” strategy is exploited to minimize the mutual interference. That is, when one analyte needs to be detected in the presence of another analyte, a sufficiently high concentration of another analyte as interfering species will be added so that its interference is amplified to a maximum and constant value. As a result, the interference will be eliminated as much as possible. Typically, when ATP needs to be quantified from mixture solution of UA and ATP, 650 μM of UA was added to the system to make the characteristic peak quenching degree of Eu3+ maximum. Then different concentrations of ATP were added and the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded. From Fig. 8a, it can be clearly observed that with increasing ATP concentration, the emission peak intensity of CDs at 440 nm gradually increased but the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ at 614 nm almost did not change longer. And the ratiometric fluorescence intensity F440/F614 showed a good linear relationship with ATP concentration in Fig. 8b. Similarly, if UA is required to be quantified, the emission peak of CDs at 440 nm is enhanced as much as possible by adding 600 μM ATP without affecting the emission peak of Eu3+ at 614 nm. As shown in Fig. 8c, the emission peak of Eu3+ at 614 nm gradually decreased but the emission peak of CDs basically maintained stable with the increase of UA concentration. It is more obvious that the change of UA concentration showed an excellent linear relationship with the relative fluorescence intensity. Consequently, UA and ATP can be quantitatively analyzed simultaneously when they co-existed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the probe material for ATP and UA
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the selectivity of the sensor for ATP and UA, fluorescence signals of the Eu/CDs@MOF-808 probe were measured for a variety of amino acids and other small biomolecules, which included D-Val, L-Val, L-His, D-His, D-Leu, L-Leu, Cre, Gly, Thr, Glu, Phe, Cys, Arg, ATP, and UA, as depicted in Fig. 9 a. The results indicated that only ATP and UA induced notable alterations in the fluorescence peaks of the Eu/CDs@MOF-808, whereas influences caused by the other substances were minimal. To emphasize the findings, a comparison of the fluorescence emission spectra before and after the introduction of ATP and UA was performed in Fig. 9b. Remarkably, the introduction of ATP resulted in a significant increase of the characteristic peak of CDs at 440 nm in the Eu/CDs@MOF-808 probe, while the peak at 614 nm corresponding to Eu3+ showed no variation. Conversely, the presence of UA caused the characteristic peak of Eu3+ at 614 nm to decrease significantly while the characteristic peak of CDs remained stable. Therefore, the developed sensor, Eu/CDs@MOF-808, can serve as a fluorescent probe for the highly selective detection of ATP, utilizing the peak of Eu3+ as a reference signal and the peak of CDs as the response signal. Likewise, ratiometric sensing for UA can be carried out by employing the characteristic peaks of CDs as a reference signal and the peaks of Eu3+ as the response signal.

The detection mechanism of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 toward UA and ATP was further analyzed. Initially, the SEM images of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 with and without ATP were compared. The SEM image of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 before ATP exposure was displayed in shown in Fig. S4a and the SEM images after ATP treatment were showed in Fig. S4b and Fig. S4c. A stark comparison indicated that the introduction of ATP notably compromised the Eu/CDs@MOF-808 framework, leading to CD release and a rise in the fluorescence peak at 440 nm. Moreover, the EDS spectrum of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 after ATP treatment (Fig. S4d) clearly revealed the presence of P element, further validating the interaction between Eu/CDs@MOF-808 and ATP. Conversely, UA's addition showed a negligible effect on the morphology of Eu/CDs@MOF-808, as depicted in Fig. S5. Therefore, the hypothesis of structural collapse leading to the attenuation of characteristic Eu3+ peaks was dismissed. Additionally, the XRD patterns of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 composites subjected to ATP and UA treatments were compared, as illustrated in Fig. S6. The XRD characteristic diffraction peaks of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 after ATP treatment changed significantly and were consistent with the SEM changes, which further suggested that the addition of ATP resulted in the breakdown of the Eu/CDs@MOF-808 structure. It is speculated that the reason for the structural collapse may be that the phosphate group in ATP specifically binds to Zr4+, thus destroying the framework structure of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 [44]. However, in spite of structural collapse, the coordination interaction between Eu3+ ion and the ligand H3BTC is not significantly affected so that characteristic emission of Eu3+ was maintained. In order to better investigate the mechanism of fluorescence enhancement by ATP, it was hypothesized that hydrogen bonding could be formed between Eu/CDs@MOFs-808 containing C-OH, C-N, and COOH functional groups and ATP, leading to the enhancement of the fluorescence intensity of Eu/CDs@MOFs-808 [45]. To verify this hypothesis, the FT-IR spectra of composites before and after ATP treatment were compared. As shown in Fig. 10 a, the FT-IR spectrum of Eu/CDs@MOFs-808 after soaking in ATP solution showed the C-OH absorption peak located at 3429 cm−1 (red line), which was blue-shifted by 41 cm−1 relative to that of the unsoaked Eu/CDs@MOFs-808 (3388 cm−1) [46]. Based on the above discussion, it can be concluded that the fluorescence enhancement of Eu/CDs@MOFs-808 may be due to the hydrogen bonding between Eu/CDs@MOFs-808 and ATP [47].

However, the presence of UA had little effect on its crystal structure and maintained the original characteristic diffraction peaks. Finally, an exploration of the fluorescence quenching mechanism of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 was conducted through theoretical calculations. At first, the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) effect was examined to confirm if the energy level of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the chromophore must surpass that of the quencher [48]. The energy levels of H3BTC and UA were calculated using density functional theory in conjunction with Gaussian 09 E.01 software. As illustrated in Fig. 10b, the LUMO level of H3BTC (−2.12 eV) was lower than that of UA (−0.911 eV), indicating that electron transfer from the LUMO of H3BTC to UA was not possible. Thus, the fluorescence quenching induced by photoinduced electron transfer (PET) in Eu/CDs@MOF-808 has been ruled out. Furthermore, the UV absorption spectra of the UA solution and the excitation spectra of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 were determined in Fig. 10c and they revealed a significant overlap. To the best of our knowledge, the quenching of Eu3+ characteristic fluorescence in Eu/CDs@MOF-808 fluorescence after the addition of UA may be caused by internal filtration effect (IFE) or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [49–51]. In order to further confirm the relevant mechanism, the fluorescence lifetimes before and after the addition of UA were recorded in Fig. 10d, and it was found that the fluorescence lifetime after the addition of UA was 252 μs, which had no significant change compared with the 250 μs of the original Eu/CDs@MOF-808. This result confirmed that the quenching mechanism may be IFE rather than FRET.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ATP and UA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate applicability of the fluorescent sensor Eu/CDs@MOF-808, it was applied to detect ATP and UA in real samples such as serum and urine. Serum and urine samples spiked with different concentrations of ATP and UA were analyzed by the standard addition method. The levels of ATP and UA in serum and urine measured by this sensor were basically consistent with the actual values in Table S4. The recoveries were in the range of 98.73–102.4 % and the RSDs were less than 3.52 %. The above results are satisfactory, indicating that the method can be employed for the determination of ATP and UA in real samples.

In conclusion, we developed a functionalized metal–organic MOF (Eu/CDs@MOF-808), which exhibited excellent stability and satisfactory luminescence properties through the introduction of CDs and rare earth ion (Eu3+) via in situ growth and post-synthetic modification strategies, respectively. The Eu/CDs@MOF-808 was utilized as a fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective determination of ATP and UA. The presence of ATP significantly enhanced the fluorescent peak of the CDs at 440 nm, while the characteristic peak of Eu3+ at 614 nm remained unchanged. Conversely, in the presence of UA, the characteristic peak of the CDs was unaffected, whereas the fluorescence intensity of Eu3+ at 614 nm was quenched. Moreover, the fluorescence response mechanism was corroborated using SEM, FT-IR, XRD, theoretical calculations, and UV absorption spectroscopy. To explore the potential of Eu/CDs@MOF-808 in real samples, we conducted ATP and UA assays in human serum and urine. This study offered an innovative approach for the detection of ATP and UA.

Liping Peng: Writing – original draft, Software, Resources, Investigation. Hao Guo: Funding acquisition. Borong Ren: Methodology. Yingfei Hui: Investigation. Dengke Zhuang: Investigation. Yu Liu: Software. Ruixue Yan: Investigation. Wenting Niu: Investigation. Wu Yang: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful to the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22364020), Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province (20JR10RA083), Northwest Normal University graduate student funding project (2023KYZZ-B059) and Key Lab of Polymer Materials of Gansu Province for their financial supports.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2024.156811.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
